title: The cerebellum and procedural memory in trauma responses
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part of your brain you didn't know was running the show

I spent years trying to think my way out of trauma. I read books. I went to therapy. I talked and talked and talked. And I kept hitting the same wall. My body would react before my mind could catch up. I'd be fine, totally fine, and then suddenly I'd be flooded. Heart racing. Muscles locked. Breath gone. And I had no idea why.

Here's the thing. I was trying to fix a problem with the wrong tool. I was using my conscious mind to solve something that wasn't stored in my conscious mind. It was stored somewhere deeper. Somewhere older. Somewhere in the back of my skull, tucked behind my brainstem, quietly running the show without asking for my permission.

I'm talking about the cerebellum.

You've probably heard of the amygdala. Everyone talks about the amygdala. It's the fear center, right? The alarm bell. But the amygdala is just the messenger. The real action, the stuff that actually runs your trauma responses, happens in a part of the brain most people have never even heard of. The cerebellum. That little cauliflower-shaped thing at the base of your skull. It's only 10% of your brain's volume, but it holds more than half of your neurons. And it's the seat of procedural memory.

Procedural memory is the memory of how to do things. Not facts. Not stories. Not "what happened." But "how to." How to ride a bike. How to tie your shoes. How to flinch when something flies at your face. And how to protect yourself from danger that happened years ago but still lives in your nervous system like it's happening right now.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets real.

What procedural memory actually is and why it matters for trauma

Think about the last time you drove a car. You didn't think about which pedal was the gas. You didn't consciously decide to turn the steering wheel. Your hands and feet just knew what to do. That's procedural memory. It's automatic. It's fast. It doesn't require thought. And it's nearly impossible to unlearn through talking alone.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He talks about how traumatic events get encoded as procedural memories. Your body learns a pattern of protection. A tensing of the shoulders. A holding of the breath. A collapse in the chest. And once that pattern is learned, it runs automatically. Every time. Without your permission. Without your awareness.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office describing a panic attack I'd had at the grocery store. I could tell you the story. I could trace it back to a memory from childhood. I could understand the cognitive link. But understanding didn't stop it from happening again. Because the understanding was in my explicit memory, my hippocampus, my narrative brain. The panic was in my cerebellum. In the procedural memory. In the part of me that doesn't use words.

Here's the hard truth. You can't talk your way out of a procedural memory. You can't reason with it. You can't explain to your cerebellum that the danger is over. Your cerebellum doesn't speak English. It speaks movement. It speaks sensation. It speaks pattern.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine walks through exactly how procedural memory gets stuck in the body and what to do about it. I read it years ago and it changed how I understood my own panic. Not because it gave me a better story. But because it gave me a way to work with the body directly.

The cerebellum and the freeze response you can't control

Let me tell you about one of the most humiliating moments of my life. I was in a conflict with someone I loved. Nothing violent. Just an argument. And I froze. Completely. My voice stopped working. My body went rigid. I couldn't move. I couldn't speak. I just sat there, staring, while the other person waited for me to respond. And I couldn't. I was locked.

Afterward, I felt ashamed. Why couldn't I just say something? Why couldn't I defend myself? I knew I was safe. I knew this person wasn't going to hurt me. But my body didn't know. My body knew something else. My body remembered a time when freezing was the safest option. And it ran that program without asking me.

That's the cerebellum at work. Research by Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," shows that trauma survivors often have altered cerebellar function. The cerebellum is involved in coordinating not just movement but also emotional responses and threat detection. When it's been shaped by trauma, it learns to overreact. It learns to freeze at the slightest hint of danger. Because in the past, freezing saved your life.

Wild, right? The same mechanism that kept you alive as a child is now the mechanism that makes you feel trapped as an adult.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk explains the neuroscience in a way that doesn't make your eyes glaze over. He talks about how trauma reshapes the brain, including the cerebellum, and what kinds of interventions actually work. It's not an easy read emotionally. But it's worth it.

Why talk therapy alone often falls short

I'm not anti-therapy. I've been in therapy for years and it's helped me enormously. But I've also seen its limits. Talk therapy works with explicit memory. It works with narrative. It helps you make sense of your story. And that's valuable. But it doesn't directly touch procedural memory.

Think about it. You can talk about a traumatic event for a hundred hours. You can reframe it. You can understand it. You can forgive the people involved. And your body can still react the same way when something triggers you. Because your cerebellum didn't attend those therapy sessions. It wasn't listening. It was busy running its old programs.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who specializes in structural dissociation, talks about this in her work. She says that trauma survivors often have parts of themselves that are stuck in the past. Not metaphorically. Literally stuck. Those parts are operating from procedural memories that haven't been updated. They don't know that the danger is over. They're still trying to protect you from a threat that no longer exists.

So what do you do? You have to work with the body. You have to work with movement. With sensation. With pattern. You have to give your cerebellum new experiences, not just new explanations.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's not a cure. Nothing is a cure. But it can take the edge off the hyperarousal that comes with procedural trauma responses. I use it on days when my nervous system feels raw. It helps me stay present enough to actually do the body-based work.

How to start working with procedural memory

I wish I could give you a simple five-step plan. I can't. This work is slow. It's nonlinear. It's frustrating. But there are things that help.

Movement is one. Not exercise for the sake of burning calories. But movement that helps you reconnect with your body in a safe way. Yoga. Tai chi. Slow walking. Shaking. Anything that gets you out of your head and into your physical self. The cerebellum learns through movement. So you have to give it new patterns to learn.

I started with something simple. I would stand up and slowly shift my weight from one foot to the other. Back and forth. For minutes at a time. It felt ridiculous. But after a while, I noticed something. My shoulders would drop. My breath would deepen. My nervous system would settle. Not because I was thinking about anything. But because I was giving my cerebellum a new pattern to follow. A pattern of safety. A pattern of ease.

Another thing that helps is pendulation. That's a term from Peter Levine's work. It means moving between a place of activation and a place of resource. You notice the tension in your body. You notice where you feel safe. And you slowly move between them. Back and forth. Like a pendulum. This teaches your nervous system that activation isn't permanent. That you can come back to safety. That the freeze doesn't have to last forever.

Deb Dana, a clinician who applies polyvagal theory in practice, talks about this as mapping your nervous system. You learn to recognize the cues of safety and the cues of danger. Not intellectually. But in your body. You learn what ventral vagal feels like. What sympathetic feels like. What dorsal feels like. And you learn how to move between them.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Dana offers exercises that are concrete and doable. Not just theory. Actual things you can try. I've used her tracking exercises for years. They've helped me notice when my cerebellum is running an old program before I get fully flooded.

What recovery looks like when you're working with procedural memory

Here's what I've learned. Recovery isn't about erasing the procedural memories. It's not about making them go away. It's about creating new ones. New patterns. New pathways. New ways of being in your body that are stronger than the old ones.

The old memories don't disappear. They're still there. But they become less dominant. They become background noise instead of the main event. You still get triggered. But you recover faster. You still freeze sometimes. But you can feel it coming and you can move before you lock up completely.

I still have moments where my body reacts before my mind can catch up. I still have panic. I still have freeze. But it's different now. I know what's happening. I know it's procedural. I know my cerebellum is just doing its job, trying to protect me from a danger that's no longer there. And that knowledge, combined with body-based practice, gives me a little bit of space. A little bit of choice.

Sit with that. You don't have to fix everything. You don't have to heal completely. You just have to create enough new patterns that the old ones don't run your life anymore.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up reading that? Did you relax? Did you hold your breath? That's your procedural memory at work. That's your cerebellum talking to you. And now you know what it is. Now you can start working with it instead of fighting it.
